{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kanda Shrine
神田明神 | image = KandaMyojin8834.jpg | caption = | width = | type = | dedication = [[Daikoku]]
[[Ebisu]]
[[Taira-no-Masakado]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 2-16-2, [[Soto-Kanda]], [[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]]
[[Tokyo]] 101-0021 | phone = +81 (03) 3254-0753 | website = http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Chiyoda, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The Shrine dates back 1,200 years, but the current structure was built sometime after the 1923 earthquake. The vermillion shrine and it's beautiful interior, lacquer and gold and Chinese-style decoration are quite impressive. {{commoncat|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} [[ja:神田明神]] {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]]

The Shrine dates back 1,200 years, but the current structure was built sometime after the 1923 earthquake. The vermillion shrine and it's beautiful interior, lacquer and gold and Chinese-style decoration are quite impressive. {{commoncat|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[ja:神田明神]] {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kanda Shrine
神田明神 | image = KandaMyojin8834.jpg | caption = | width = | type = | dedication = [[Daikoku]]
[[Ebisu]]
[[Taira-no-Masakado]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 2-16-2, [[Soto-Kanda]], [[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]]
[[Tokyo]] 101-0021 | phone = +81 (03) 3254-0753 | website = http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin| formerly 神田神社 ''Kanda-jinja''}}, is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Chiyoda, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda shrine was an important shrine to both the warrior class and citizens of Japan, especially during the [[Edo period]], when [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] paid his respects at Kanda shrine. == History == Kanda shrine was first built in the second year of the [[Tenpyō]] Era (730 [[AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern [[Ōtemachi]] district. In order to accommodate the expansion of [[Edo Castle]], the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near [[Akihabara]] in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[WWII]]. Unlike many of Japan's historical structures, restoration is being done on Kanda shrine, and work continues today. == Architecture == [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon, marks the entrance to Kanda shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. == Enshrined Kami == The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. == Festivals == {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival ([[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[Battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda shrine in January. == Cultural References == Kanda shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. == Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Laterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji == External Links == *[http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html Official Site] (English) {{commoncat|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[ja:神田明神]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]]

Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. == Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Laterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji == External Links == *[http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html Official Site] (English) {{commoncat|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[ja:神田明神]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]]

The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[WWII]]. Unlike many of Japan's historical structures, restoration is being done on Kanda shrine, and work continues today. == Architecture == [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon (隨神門)]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. == Enshrined Kami == The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. == Festivals == {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival ([[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda shrine in January. == Cultural References == Kanda shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. == Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Laterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji == External Links == *[http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html Official Site] (English) {{commoncat|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[ja:神田明神]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]]

The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the warrior class and citizens of Japan, especially during the [[Edo period]], when [[shogun]] [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] paid his respects at Kanda Shrine. == History == Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the [[Tenpyō]] Era (730 [[AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern [[Ōtemachi]] district. In order to accommodate the expansion of [[Edo Castle]], the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near [[Akihabara]] in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[WWII]]. Unlike many of Japan's historical structures, restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. == Architecture == [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. == Enshrined Kami == The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. == Festivals == {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (''Kanda'' [[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. == Cultural References == Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. == Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji == External links == *[http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html Official Site] (English) {{commoncat|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[zh:神田明神]]

Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html Official Site] (English) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[zh:神田明神]]

{{No footnotes|date=January 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kanda Shrine
神田明神 | image = KandaMyojin8834.jpg | caption = The Kanda Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | width = | type = | dedication = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]] ([[Daikoku]])
[[Sukunabikona]] ([[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]])
[[Taira no Masakado]] | founded = 730 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 2-16-2, [[Soto-Kanda]], [[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]], [[Tokyo]]
[[〒]] 101-0021 | phone = +81 (03) 3254-0753 | website = http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin| formerly 神田神社 ''Kanda-jinja''}}, is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Chiyoda, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the warrior class and citizens of Japan, especially during the [[Edo period]], when [[shogun]] [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] paid his respects at Kanda Shrine. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the [[Tenpyō]] Era (730 [[AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern [[Ōtemachi]] district. In order to accommodate the expansion of [[Edo Castle]], the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near [[Akihabara]] in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[WWII]]. Unlike many of Japan's historical structures, restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (''Kanda'' [[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html Official Site] (English) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[fa:معبد کاندا]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[zh:神田明神]]

The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the warrior class and citizens of Japan, especially during the [[Edo period]], when [[shogun]] [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] paid his respects at Kanda Shrine. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the [[Tenpyō]] Era (730 [[AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern [[Ōtemachi]] district. In order to accommodate the expansion of [[Edo Castle]], the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near [[Akihabara]] in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[World War II]], unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[World War II]], unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (''Kanda'' [[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html Official Site] (English) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]]

[[〒]] 101-0021 | phone = +81 (03) 3254-0753 | website = http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/english/top.html }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin| formerly 神田神社 ''Kanda-jinja''}}, is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Chiyoda, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the warrior class and citizens of Japan, especially during the [[Edo period]], when [[shogun]] [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] paid his respects at Kanda Shrine. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the [[Tenpyō]] Era (730 [[AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern [[Ōtemachi]] district. In order to accommodate the expansion of [[Edo Castle]], the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near [[Akihabara]] in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[World War II]], unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (''Kanda'' [[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the fictional [[anime]] titled ''[[Love Live!]]'' the character of Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamoujin shrine. Announced via [[Twitter]] in early 2015, the administrators of the Kandamyoujin shrine have in turn adopted the Nozomi Tojo character as the official mascot of the shrine.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine announces adoption of anime character as official mascot|url=https://twitter.com/kanda_myoujin/status/551083827108380673|website=Twitter.com|date=2015-01-02}} Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]]

Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (''Kanda'' [[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]]

The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the warrior class and citizens of Japan, especially during the [[Edo period]], when [[shogun]] [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] paid his respects at Kanda Shrine. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the [[Tenpyō]] Era (730 [[AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern [[Ōtemachi]] district. In order to accommodate the expansion of [[Edo Castle]], the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near [[Akihabara]] in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[World War II]], unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due in part to the proximity of the present-day Kandamyoujin shrine to [[Akihabara|Akihabara Electric Town]], the shrine has become a mecca for the [[technophilia|technophiles]] who frequent Akihabara. The Kandamyoujin shrine sells [[omamori|talismans]] specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp}} ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (''Kanda'' [[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the fictional [[anime]] titled ''[[Love Live!]]'' the character of Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine. Announced via [[Twitter]] in early 2015, the administrators of the Kandamyoujin shrine have in turn adopted the Nozomi Tojo character as the official mascot of the shrine.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine announces adoption of anime character as official mascot|url=https://twitter.com/kanda_myoujin/status/551083827108380673|website=Twitter.com|date=2015-01-02}} Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) * [https://twitter.com/kanda_myoujin Official Twitter feed] (Japanese) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]]

Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (''Kanda'' [[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the fictional [[anime]] titled ''[[Love Live!]]'' the character of Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine. Announced via [[Twitter]] in early 2015, the administrators of the Kandamyoujin shrine have in turn adopted the Nozomi Tojo character as the official mascot of the shrine.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine announces adoption of anime character as official mascot|url=https://twitter.com/kanda_myoujin/status/551083827108380673|website=Twitter.com|date=2015-01-02}} Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) * [https://twitter.com/kanda_myoujin Official Twitter feed] (Japanese) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]]

{{More footnotes|date=January 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kanda Shrine
神田明神 | image = KandaMyojin8834.jpg | caption = The Kanda Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | width = | type = | dedication = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]] ([[Daikoku]])
[[Sukunabikona]] ([[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]])
[[Taira no Masakado]] | founded = 730 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 2-16-2, [[Soto-Kanda]], [[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]], [[Tokyo]]
[[〒]] 101-0021 | phone = +81 (03) 3254-0753 | website = http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin|formerly 神田神社 ''Kanda-jinja''}}, is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Chiyoda, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the warrior class and citizens of Japan, especially during the [[Edo period]], when [[shogun]] [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] paid his respects at Kanda Shrine.{{Citation needed|date=February 2016}} Due in part to the proximity of the Kanda shrine to [[Akihabara]], the shrine has become a mecca for the [[technophilia|technophiles]] who frequent Akihabara. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the [[Tenpyō]] Era (730 [[AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern [[Ōtemachi]] district. In order to accommodate the expansion of [[Edo Castle]], the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near [[Akihabara]] in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[World War II]], unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due in part to the proximity of the present-day Kandamyoujin shrine to [[Akihabara|Akihabara Electric Town]], the shrine has become a mecca for the [[technophilia|technophiles]] who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda shrine sells [[omamori|talismans]] specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp}} ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji|emperor]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (''Kanda'' [[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the fictional [[anime]] titled ''[[Love Live!]]'' the character of Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine. Announced via [[Twitter]] in early 2015, the administrators of the Kandamyoujin shrine have in turn adopted the Nozomi Tojo character as the official mascot of the shrine.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine announces adoption of anime character as official mascot|url=https://twitter.com/kanda_myoujin/status/551083827108380673|website=Twitter.com|date=2015-01-02}} Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) * [https://twitter.com/kanda_myoujin Official Twitter feed] (Japanese) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]]

Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (''Kanda'' [[Japanese festivals|''matsuri'']]) is one of the three major Shinto festivals in Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Enjoy The Kanda Festival In Tokyo! {{!}} MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|url=http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|website=MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|accessdate=3 September 2017|language=ja|date=17 April 2017}} Due in part to the proximity of the Kanda Shrine to [[Akihabara]], the shrine has become a mecca for the [[technophilia|technophiles]] who frequent Akihabara. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the [[Tenpyō]] Era (730 [[AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern [[Ōtemachi]] district. In order to accommodate the expansion of [[Edo Castle]], the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near [[Akihabara]] in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[World War II]], unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due to its proximity to the [[Akihabara|Akihabara Electric Town]], the shrine has become a mecca for the [[technophilia|technophiles]] who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda Shrine sells [[omamori|talismans]] specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp}} ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[Festivals in Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle so that even the shogun could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the fictional [[anime]] titled ''[[Love Live!]]'' the character of Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine. Announced via [[Twitter]] in early 2015, the administrators of the Kandamyoujin shrine have in turn adopted the Nozomi Tojo character as the official mascot of the shrine.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine announces adoption of anime character as official mascot|url=https://twitter.com/kanda_myoujin/status/551083827108380673|website=Twitter.com|date=2015-01-02}} Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) * [https://twitter.com/kanda_myoujin Official Twitter feed] (Japanese) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]]

Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]]

{{More footnotes|date=January 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kanda Shrine
神田明神 | image = Kanda-Myojin 2012.JPG | caption = The Kanda Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | width = | type = | dedication = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi]] ([[Daikoku]])
[[Sukunabikona]] ([[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]])
[[Taira no Masakado]] | founded = 730 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 2-16-2, [[Soto-Kanda]], [[Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]], [[Tokyo]]
[[〒]] 101-0021 | phone = +81 (03) 3254-0753 | website = http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin|officially 神田神社 ''Kanda-jinja''}}, is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Chiyoda, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the [[Samurai|warrior]] class and citizens of Japan, especially during the [[Edo period]], when ''[[shōgun]]'' [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] paid his respects at Kanda Shrine.{{cite web|title=Visiting Japan In May? Enjoy The Kanda Festival In Tokyo! {{!}} MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|url=http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|website=MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|accessdate=3 September 2017|language=ja|date=17 April 2017}} Due in part to the proximity of the Kanda Shrine to [[Akihabara]], the shrine has become a mecca for the [[technophilia|technophiles]] who frequent Akihabara. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the [[Tenpyō]] Era (730 [[AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern [[Ōtemachi]] district. In order to accommodate the expansion of [[Edo Castle]], the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near [[Akihabara]] in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo [[Bombing of Tokyo|firebombing]] of [[World War II]], unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due to its proximity to the [[Akihabara|Akihabara Electric Town]], the shrine has become a mecca for the [[technophilia|technophiles]] who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda Shrine sells [[omamori|talismans]] specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp}} ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[Festivals in Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the fictional [[anime]] titled ''[[Love Live!]]'' the character of Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine. Announced via [[Twitter]] in early 2015, the administrators of the Kandamyoujin shrine have in turn adopted the Nozomi Tojo character as the official mascot of the shrine.{{cite tweet|user=kanda_myoujin|author=神田明神|number=551083827108380673|date=2 January 2015|title=TOKYO MXテレビで、アニメ「ラブライブ！」が、1月4日23時から放送されます。 神田明神公式アイドルの「東條 希」が出演していて、神田明神もアニメ中に出てきます。 僕、ししまいも応援していますよm(_ _)m}} Kanda Shrine is popularly known as the hangout for [[Zenigata Heiji]], a fictional policeman who thwarted criminals by throwing coins. Heiji's beat is ''Myōjin-shita'', or "beneath the (Kanda) Myōjin shrine." Several monuments have been erected at the shrine in his honor. ==Images== File:Daikoku8727.jpg|Statue of Daikoku File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|[[Chōzuya]] Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{coord|35|42|07|N|139|46|04|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]]

The Kanda Shrine sells [[omamori|talismans]] specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp}} ==Architecture== [[Image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with [[cypress]] wood, and is built with an [[irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], [[Ebisu (mythology)|Ebisu]], and [[Taira no Masakado]]. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[Seven Gods of Fortune]], Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado however, was a [[samurai]] who rebelled against the [[Heian period|Heian]] government, and was later elevated to the status of kami out of reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his death in 940, his head was separated from his body and delivered to the Shibaraki area, near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the [[Meiji period]], the [[Emperor Meiji]] was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as an anti-government figure. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after WWII. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[Festivals in Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the [[battle of Sekigahara]]. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every [[Parity (mathematics)|odd]] year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the fictional [[anime]] titled ''[[Love Live!]]'' the character of Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine.
