However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]]. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[:en:Festivals of Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every {{ill|Parity (mathematics)|en|Parity (mathematics)|WD=Q230967|lt=odd|1=Parity (mathematics)}} year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the {{ill|Anime|en|Anime|WD=Q1107|lt=Anime|1=Anime}} and multimedia franchise ''{{ill|Love Live! School Idol Project|en|Love Live! School Idol Project|WD=Q3280971|lt=Love Live! School Idol Project|1=Love Live! School Idol Project}}'', the character Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine, while she and the other members of protagonist idol group μ's train by running up and down stairs leading to the shrine. In 2015, the shrine's administrators named Nozomi as an official mascot.{{cite tweet|user=kanda_myoujin|author=神田明神|number=551083827108380673|date=2 January 2015|title=TOKYO MXテレビで、アニメ「ラブライブ！」が、1月4日23時から放送されます。 神田明神公式アイドルの「東條 希」が出演していて、神田明神もアニメ中に出てきます。 僕、ししまいも応援していますよm(_ _)m}} ==images== File:Kanda-Myojin torii.JPG|[[Torii]] File:Daikoku8727.jpg|[[Daikokuten]] File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji File:Kanda Matsuri 2009-1 in Akihabara.jpg|{{ill|Kanda Matsuri|en|Kanda Matsuri|WD=Q218646|lt=Kanda Matsuri|1=Kanda Matsuri}} File:100 views edo 010.jpg|[[Hiroshige]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons Category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] [[en:Kanda Shrine]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]] {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin}} [[de:Kanda Myōjin]] [[eu:Kanda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد کاندا]] [[fr:Kanda-myōjin]] [[id:Kuil Kanda]] [[it:Santuario di Kanda]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[pl:Kanda-myōjin]] [[zh:神田明神]] {{wikidata link|Q717682}} [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiyoda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1934]] [[Category:Shinto in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[arz:كاندا ميوجين]][[co:commons:Category:Kanda-Myojin]][[de:Kanda Myōjin]][[en:Kanda Shrine]][[eu:Kanda santutegia]][[fa:معبد کاندا]][[fr:Kanda-myōjin]][[id:Kuil Kanda]][[it:Santuario di Kanda]][[ja:神田明神]][[pl:Kanda-myōjin]][[simple:Kanda Myōjin Shrine]][[zh:神田明神]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]][[co:commons:Category:Kanda-Myojin]][[de:Kanda Myōjin]][[en:Kanda Shrine]][[eu:Kanda santutegia]][[fa:معبد کاندا]][[fr:Kanda-myōjin]][[id:Kuil Kanda]][[it:Santuario di Kanda]][[ja:神田明神]][[pl:Kanda-myōjin]][[simple:Kanda Myōjin Shrine]][[zh:神田明神]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]][[co:commons:Category:Kanda-Myojin]][[de:Kanda Myōjin]][[en:Kanda Shrine]][[eu:Kanda santutegia]][[fa:معبد کاندا]][[fr:Kanda-myōjin]][[id:Kuil Kanda]][[it:Santuario di Kanda]][[ja:神田明神]][[pl:Kanda-myōjin]][[simple:Kanda Myōjin Shrine]][[zh:神田明神]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]][[Category:sn_crud commons:Category:Kanda-Myojin]][[de:Kanda Myōjin]][[en:Kanda Shrine]][[eu:Kanda santutegia]][[fa:معبد کاندا]][[fr:Kanda-myōjin]][[id:Kuil Kanda]][[it:Santuario di Kanda]][[ja:神田明神]][[pl:Kanda-myōjin]][[simple:Kanda Myōjin Shrine]][[zh:神田明神]] {{More footnotes|date=January 2011}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kanda Shrine
神田明神 | image = File:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg | caption = The Kanda Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.701944|N|139.767778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}}; {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = 730 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-16-2, [[:en:Soto-Kanda|Soto-Kanda]], {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|WD=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 101-0021 | website = {{URL|http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Soto-Kanda|WD=Q3347175|1=Soto-Kanda}} | part_of = {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|WD=Q164895|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin|officially 神田神社 ''Kanda-jinja''}}, is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|WD=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda, Tokyo|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=warrior|1=Samurai}} class and citizens of Japan, especially during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, when ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} paid his respects at Kanda Shrine.{{cite web|title=Visiting Japan In May? Enjoy The Kanda Festival In Tokyo! {{!}} MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|url=http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|website=MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|access-date=3 September 2017|language=ja|date=17 April 2017|archive-date=3 September 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903121213/http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|url-status=live}} Due in part to the proximity of the Kanda Shrine to {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|WD=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} Era (730 [[:en:AD|AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern {{ill|Ōtemachi|en|Ōtemachi|WD=Q1332496|lt=Ōtemachi|1=Ōtemachi}} district. In order to accommodate the expansion of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}, the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara|1=Akihabara}} in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|WD=Q59886|lt=firebombing|1=Bombing of Tokyo}} of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due to its proximity to the {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara Electric Town|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for the {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|WD=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda Shrine sells {{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|WD=Q1756788|lt=talismans|1=Omamori}} specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp|access-date=2015-12-05|archive-date=2015-12-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151208062432/http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|url-status=live}} ==Architecture== The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|WD=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} wood, and is built with an [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, and {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}}. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[:en:Seven Gods of Fortune|Seven Gods of Fortune]] (Japanese, "Shichifukujin"), Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado was a land-owning government official who led a massive insurrection against the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} government and declared himself the "New Emperor" (新皇). He was later elevated to the status of a local kami out of a mixture of fear and reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his defeat in 940 AD, he was {{ill|Decapitation|en|Decapitation|WD=Q204933|lt=decapitated|1=Decapitation}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Hidesato|en|Fujiwara no Hidesato|WD=Q1136623|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}} and his severed head was brought to the Shibasaki ({{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}) area in a wooden bucket (首桶) and buried on a low hill near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance, and fearing his curse, enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as a dangerous anti-government {{ill|Demagogue|en|Demagogue|WD=Q10264925|lt=Demagogue|1=Demagogue}}. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]]. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[:en:Festivals of Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every {{ill|Parity (mathematics)|en|Parity (mathematics)|WD=Q230967|lt=odd|1=Parity (mathematics)}} year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the {{ill|Anime|en|Anime|WD=Q1107|lt=Anime|1=Anime}} and multimedia franchise ''{{ill|Love Live! School Idol Project|en|Love Live! School Idol Project|WD=Q3280971|lt=Love Live! School Idol Project|1=Love Live! School Idol Project}}'', the character Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine, while she and the other members of protagonist idol group μ's train by running up and down stairs leading to the shrine. In 2015, the shrine's administrators named Nozomi as an official mascot.{{cite tweet|user=kanda_myoujin|author=神田明神|number=551083827108380673|date=2 January 2015|title=TOKYO MXテレビで、アニメ「ラブライブ！」が、1月4日23時から放送されます。 神田明神公式アイドルの「東條 希」が出演していて、神田明神もアニメ中に出てきます。 僕、ししまいも応援していますよm(_ _)m}} ==images== File:Kanda-Myojin torii.JPG|[[Torii]] File:Daikoku8727.jpg|[[Daikokuten]] File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji File:Kanda Matsuri 2009-1 in Akihabara.jpg|{{ill|Kanda Matsuri|en|Kanda Matsuri|WD=Q218646|lt=Kanda Matsuri|1=Kanda Matsuri}} File:100 views edo 010.jpg|[[Hiroshige]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons Category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] [[en:Kanda Shrine]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]] {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin}} [[de:Kanda Myōjin]] [[eu:Kanda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد کاندا]] [[fr:Kanda-myōjin]] [[id:Kuil Kanda]] [[it:Santuario di Kanda]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[pl:Kanda-myōjin]] [[zh:神田明神]] {{wikidata link|Q717682}} [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiyoda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1934]] [[Category:Shinto in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due to its proximity to the {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara Electric Town|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for the {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|WD=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda Shrine sells {{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|WD=Q1756788|lt=talismans|1=Omamori}} specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp|access-date=2015-12-05|archive-date=2015-12-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151208062432/http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|url-status=live}} ==Architecture== The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|WD=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} wood, and is built with an [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, and {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}}. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[:en:Seven Gods of Fortune|Seven Gods of Fortune]] (Japanese, "Shichifukujin"), Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado was a land-owning government official who led a massive insurrection against the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} government and declared himself the "New Emperor" (新皇). He was later elevated to the status of a local kami out of a mixture of fear and reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his defeat in 940 AD, he was {{ill|Decapitation|en|Decapitation|WD=Q204933|lt=decapitated|1=Decapitation}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Hidesato|en|Fujiwara no Hidesato|WD=Q1136623|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}} and his severed head was brought to the Shibasaki ({{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}) area in a wooden bucket (首桶) and buried on a low hill near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance, and fearing his curse, enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as a dangerous anti-government {{ill|Demagogue|en|Demagogue|WD=Q10264925|lt=Demagogue|1=Demagogue}}. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]]. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[:en:Festivals of Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every {{ill|Parity (mathematics)|en|Parity (mathematics)|WD=Q230967|lt=odd|1=Parity (mathematics)}} year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the {{ill|Anime|en|Anime|WD=Q1107|lt=Anime|1=Anime}} and multimedia franchise ''{{ill|Love Live! School Idol Project|en|Love Live! School Idol Project|WD=Q3280971|lt=Love Live! School Idol Project|1=Love Live! School Idol Project}}'', the character Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine, while she and the other members of protagonist idol group μ's train by running up and down stairs leading to the shrine. In 2015, the shrine's administrators named Nozomi as an official mascot.{{cite tweet|user=kanda_myoujin|author=神田明神|number=551083827108380673|date=2 January 2015|title=TOKYO MXテレビで、アニメ「ラブライブ！」が、1月4日23時から放送されます。 神田明神公式アイドルの「東條 希」が出演していて、神田明神もアニメ中に出てきます。 僕、ししまいも応援していますよm(_ _)m}} ==images== File:Kanda-Myojin torii.JPG|[[Torii]] File:Daikoku8727.jpg|[[Daikokuten]] File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji File:Kanda Matsuri 2009-1 in Akihabara.jpg|{{ill|Kanda Matsuri|en|Kanda Matsuri|WD=Q218646|lt=Kanda Matsuri|1=Kanda Matsuri}} File:100 views edo 010.jpg|[[Hiroshige]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons Category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] [[en:Kanda Shrine]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]] {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin}} [[de:Kanda Myōjin]] [[eu:Kanda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد کاندا]] [[fr:Kanda-myōjin]] [[id:Kuil Kanda]] [[it:Santuario di Kanda]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[pl:Kanda-myōjin]] [[zh:神田明神]] {{wikidata link|Q717682}} [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiyoda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1934]] [[Category:Shinto in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[arz:كاندا ميوجين]][[co:commons:Category:Kanda-Myojin]][[de:Kanda Myōjin]][[en:Kanda Shrine]][[eu:Kanda santutegia]][[fa:معبد کاندا]][[fr:Kanda-myōjin]][[id:Kuil Kanda]][[it:Santuario di Kanda]][[ja:神田明神]][[pl:Kanda-myōjin]][[simple:Kanda Myōjin Shrine]][[zh:神田明神]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]][[co:commons:Category:Kanda-Myojin]][[de:Kanda Myōjin]][[en:Kanda Shrine]][[eu:Kanda santutegia]][[fa:معبد کاندا]][[fr:Kanda-myōjin]][[id:Kuil Kanda]][[it:Santuario di Kanda]][[ja:神田明神]][[pl:Kanda-myōjin]][[simple:Kanda Myōjin Shrine]][[zh:神田明神]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]][[co:commons:Category:Kanda-Myojin]][[de:Kanda Myōjin]][[en:Kanda Shrine]][[eu:Kanda santutegia]][[fa:معبد کاندا]][[fr:Kanda-myōjin]][[id:Kuil Kanda]][[it:Santuario di Kanda]][[ja:神田明神]][[pl:Kanda-myōjin]][[simple:Kanda Myōjin Shrine]][[zh:神田明神]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kanda-Myojin]][[de:Kanda Myōjin]][[en:Kanda Shrine]][[eu:Kanda santutegia]][[fa:معبد کاندا]][[fr:Kanda-myōjin]][[id:Kuil Kanda]][[it:Santuario di Kanda]][[ja:神田明神]][[pl:Kanda-myōjin]][[simple:Kanda Myōjin Shrine]][[zh:神田明神]] {{More footnotes|date=January 2011}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kanda Shrine
神田明神 | image = File:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg | caption = The Kanda Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.701944|N|139.767778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}}; {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = 730 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-16-2, [[:en:Soto-Kanda|Soto-Kanda]], {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|WD=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 101-0021 | website = {{URL|http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Soto-Kanda|WD=Q3347175|1=Soto-Kanda}} | part_of = {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|WD=Q164895|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin|officially 神田神社 ''Kanda-jinja''}}, is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|WD=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda, Tokyo|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=warrior|1=Samurai}} class and citizens of Japan, especially during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, when ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} paid his respects at Kanda Shrine.{{cite web|title=Visiting Japan In May? Enjoy The Kanda Festival In Tokyo! {{!}} MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|url=http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|website=MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|access-date=3 September 2017|language=ja|date=17 April 2017|archive-date=3 September 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903121213/http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|url-status=live}} Due in part to the proximity of the Kanda Shrine to {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|WD=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} Era (730 [[:en:AD|AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern {{ill|Ōtemachi|en|Ōtemachi|WD=Q1332496|lt=Ōtemachi|1=Ōtemachi}} district. In order to accommodate the expansion of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}, the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara|1=Akihabara}} in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|WD=Q59886|lt=firebombing|1=Bombing of Tokyo}} of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due to its proximity to the {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara Electric Town|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for the {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|WD=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda Shrine sells {{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|WD=Q1756788|lt=talismans|1=Omamori}} specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp|access-date=2015-12-05|archive-date=2015-12-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151208062432/http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|url-status=live}} ==Architecture== The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|WD=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} wood, and is built with an [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, and {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}}. Taira no Masakado was a land-owning government official who led a massive insurrection against the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} government and declared himself the "New Emperor" (新皇). He was later elevated to the status of a local kami out of a mixture of fear and reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his defeat in 940 AD, he was {{ill|Decapitation|en|Decapitation|WD=Q204933|lt=decapitated|1=Decapitation}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Hidesato|en|Fujiwara no Hidesato|WD=Q1136623|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}} and his severed head was brought to the Shibasaki ({{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}) area in a wooden bucket (首桶) and buried on a low hill near the shrine's location today. It is also said that Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as a dangerous anti-government {{ill|Demagogue|en|Demagogue|WD=Q10264925|lt=Demagogue|1=Demagogue}}. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]]. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[:en:Festivals of Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every {{ill|Parity (mathematics)|en|Parity (mathematics)|WD=Q230967|lt=odd|1=Parity (mathematics)}} year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the {{ill|Anime|en|Anime|WD=Q1107|lt=Anime|1=Anime}} and multimedia franchise ''{{ill|Love Live! School Idol Project|en|Love Live! School Idol Project|WD=Q3280971|lt=Love Live! School Idol Project|1=Love Live! School Idol Project}}'', the character Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine, while she and the other members of protagonist idol group μ's train by running up and down stairs leading to the shrine. In 2015, the shrine's administrators named Nozomi as an official mascot.{{cite tweet|user=kanda_myoujin|author=神田明神|number=551083827108380673|date=2 January 2015|title=TOKYO MXテレビで、アニメ「ラブライブ！」が、1月4日23時から放送されます。 神田明神公式アイドルの「東條 希」が出演していて、神田明神もアニメ中に出てきます。 僕、ししまいも応援していますよm(_ _)m}} ==images== File:Kanda-Myojin torii.JPG|[[Torii]] File:Daikoku8727.jpg|[[Daikokuten]] File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji File:Kanda Matsuri 2009-1 in Akihabara.jpg|{{ill|Kanda Matsuri|en|Kanda Matsuri|WD=Q218646|lt=Kanda Matsuri|1=Kanda Matsuri}} File:100 views edo 010.jpg|[[Hiroshige]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons Category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] [[en:Kanda Shrine]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]] {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin}} [[de:Kanda Myōjin]] [[eu:Kanda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد کاندا]] [[fr:Kanda-myōjin]] [[id:Kuil Kanda]] [[it:Santuario di Kanda]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[pl:Kanda-myōjin]] [[zh:神田明神]] {{wikidata link|Q717682}} [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiyoda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1934]] [[Category:Shinto in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

神田明神 | image = File:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg | caption = The Kanda Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.701944|N|139.767778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}}; {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = 730 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-16-2, [[:en:Soto-Kanda|Soto-Kanda]], {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|WD=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 101-0021 | website = {{URL|http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Soto-Kanda|WD=Q3347175|1=Soto-Kanda}} | part_of = {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|WD=Q164895|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin|officially 神田神社 ''Kanda-jinja''}}, is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|WD=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda, Tokyo|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=warrior|1=Samurai}} class and citizens of Japan, especially during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, when ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} paid his respects at Kanda Shrine.{{cite web|title=Visiting Japan In May? Enjoy The Kanda Festival In Tokyo! {{!}} MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|url=http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|website=MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|access-date=3 September 2017|language=ja|date=17 April 2017|archive-date=3 September 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903121213/http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|url-status=live}} Due in part to the proximity of the Kanda Shrine to {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|WD=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} Era (730 [[:en:AD|AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern {{ill|Ōtemachi|en|Ōtemachi|WD=Q1332496|lt=Ōtemachi|1=Ōtemachi}} district. In order to accommodate the expansion of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}, the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara|1=Akihabara}} in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|WD=Q59886|lt=firebombing|1=Bombing of Tokyo}} of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due to its proximity to the {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|WD=Q418096|lt=Akihabara Electric Town|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for the {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|WD=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda Shrine sells {{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|WD=Q1756788|lt=talismans|1=Omamori}} specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp|access-date=2015-12-05|archive-date=2015-12-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151208062432/http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|url-status=live}} ==Architecture== The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|WD=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} wood, and is built with an [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, and {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}}. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[:en:Seven Gods of Fortune|Seven Gods of Fortune]] (Japanese, "Shichifukujin"), Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado was a land-owning government official who led a massive insurrection against the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} government and declared himself the "New Emperor" (新皇). He was later elevated to the status of a local kami out of a mixture of fear and reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his defeat in 940 AD, he was {{ill|Decapitation|en|Decapitation|WD=Q204933|lt=decapitated|1=Decapitation}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Hidesato|en|Fujiwara no Hidesato|WD=Q1136623|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}} and his severed head was brought to the Shibasaki ({{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}) area in a wooden bucket (首桶) and buried on a low hill near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance, and fearing his curse, enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as a dangerous anti-government {{ill|Demagogue|en|Demagogue|WD=Q10264925|lt=Demagogue|1=Demagogue}}. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]]. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[:en:Festivals of Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every {{ill|Parity (mathematics)|en|Parity (mathematics)|WD=Q230967|lt=odd|1=Parity (mathematics)}} year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the {{ill|Anime|en|Anime|WD=Q1107|lt=Anime|1=Anime}} and multimedia franchise ''{{ill|Love Live! School Idol Project|en|Love Live! School Idol Project|WD=Q3280971|lt=Love Live! School Idol Project|1=Love Live! School Idol Project}}'', the character Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine, while she and the other members of protagonist idol group μ's train by running up and down stairs leading to the shrine. In 2015, the shrine's administrators named Nozomi as an official mascot.{{cite tweet|user=kanda_myoujin|author=神田明神|number=551083827108380673|date=2 January 2015|title=TOKYO MXテレビで、アニメ「ラブライブ！」が、1月4日23時から放送されます。 神田明神公式アイドルの「東條 希」が出演していて、神田明神もアニメ中に出てきます。 僕、ししまいも応援していますよm(_ _)m}} ==images== File:Kanda-Myojin torii.JPG|[[Torii]] File:Daikoku8727.jpg|[[Daikokuten]] File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji File:Kanda Matsuri 2009-1 in Akihabara.jpg|{{ill|Kanda Matsuri|en|Kanda Matsuri|WD=Q218646|lt=Kanda Matsuri|1=Kanda Matsuri}} File:100 views edo 010.jpg|[[Hiroshige]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons Category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] [[en:Kanda Shrine]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]] {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin}} [[de:Kanda Myōjin]] [[eu:Kanda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد کاندا]] [[fr:Kanda-myōjin]] [[id:Kuil Kanda]] [[it:Santuario di Kanda]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[pl:Kanda-myōjin]] [[zh:神田明神]] {{wikidata link|Q717682}} [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiyoda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1934]] [[Category:Shinto in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=warrior|1=Samurai}} class and citizens of Japan, especially during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, when ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} paid his respects at Kanda Shrine.{{cite web|title=Visiting Japan In May? Enjoy The Kanda Festival In Tokyo! {{!}} MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|url=http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|website=MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|access-date=3 September 2017|language=ja|date=17 April 2017|archive-date=3 September 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903121213/http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|url-status=live}} Due in part to the proximity of the Kanda Shrine to {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|qid=Q418096|lt=Akihabara|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|qid=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|qid=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} Era (730 [[:en:AD|AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern {{ill|Ōtemachi|en|Ōtemachi|qid=Q1332496|lt=Ōtemachi|1=Ōtemachi}} district. Due to its proximity to the {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|qid=Q418096|lt=Akihabara Electric Town|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for the {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|qid=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda Shrine sells {{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|qid=Q1756788|lt=talismans|1=Omamori}} specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp|access-date=2015-12-05|archive-date=2015-12-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151208062432/http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|url-status=live}} ==Architecture== The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|qid=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} wood, and is built with an [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, and {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|qid=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}}. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[:en:Seven Gods of Fortune|Seven Gods of Fortune]] (Japanese, "Shichifukujin"), Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado was a land-owning government official who led a massive insurrection against the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} government and declared himself the "New Emperor" (新皇). He was later elevated to the status of a local kami out of a mixture of fear and reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his defeat in 940 AD, he was {{ill|Decapitation|en|Decapitation|qid=Q204933|lt=decapitated|1=Decapitation}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Hidesato|en|Fujiwara no Hidesato|qid=Q1136623|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}} and his severed head was brought to the Shibasaki ({{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}) area in a wooden bucket (首桶) and buried on a low hill near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance, and fearing his curse, enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as a dangerous anti-government {{ill|Demagogue|en|Demagogue|qid=Q10264925|lt=Demagogue|1=Demagogue}}. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]]. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[:en:Festivals of Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|qid=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every {{ill|Parity (mathematics)|en|Parity (mathematics)|qid=Q230967|lt=odd|1=Parity (mathematics)}} year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the {{ill|Anime|en|Anime|qid=Q1107|lt=Anime|1=Anime}} and multimedia franchise ''{{ill|Love Live! School Idol Project|en|Love Live! School Idol Project|qid=Q3280971|lt=Love Live! School Idol Project|1=Love Live! School Idol Project}}'', the character Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine, while she and the other members of protagonist idol group μ's train by running up and down stairs leading to the shrine. In 2015, the shrine's administrators named Nozomi as an official mascot.{{cite tweet|user=kanda_myoujin|author=神田明神|number=551083827108380673|date=2 January 2015|title=TOKYO MXテレビで、アニメ「ラブライブ！」が、1月4日23時から放送されます。 神田明神公式アイドルの「東條 希」が出演していて、神田明神もアニメ中に出てきます。 僕、ししまいも応援していますよm(_ _)m}} ==images== File:Kanda-Myojin torii.JPG|[[Torii]] File:Daikoku8727.jpg|[[Daikokuten]] File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji File:Kanda Matsuri 2009-1 in Akihabara.jpg|{{ill|Kanda Matsuri|en|Kanda Matsuri|qid=Q218646|lt=Kanda Matsuri|1=Kanda Matsuri}} File:100 views edo 010.jpg|[[Hiroshige]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons Category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] [[en:Kanda Shrine]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]] {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin}} [[de:Kanda Myōjin]] [[eu:Kanda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد کاندا]] [[fr:Kanda-myōjin]] [[id:Kuil Kanda]] [[it:Santuario di Kanda]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[pl:Kanda-myōjin]] [[zh:神田明神]] {{wikidata link|Q717682}} [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiyoda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1934]] [[Category:Shinto in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

神田明神 | image = File:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg | caption = The Kanda Shrine ''[[honden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.701944|N|139.767778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona|qid=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}}; {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|qid=Q1207652}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = 730 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-16-2, [[:en:Soto-Kanda|Soto-Kanda]], {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|qid=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 101-0021 | website = {{URL|http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|qid=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Soto-Kanda|qid=Q3347175|1=Soto-Kanda}} | part_of = {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|qid=Q164895|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kanda Shrine'''|神田明神|Kanda-myōjin|officially 神田神社 ''Kanda-jinja''}}, is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|qid=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda, Tokyo|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine dates back 1,270 years, but the current structure was rebuilt several times due to fire and earthquakes. It is situated in one of the most expensive estate areas of Tokyo. Kanda Shrine was an important shrine to both the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=warrior|1=Samurai}} class and citizens of Japan, especially during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, when ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} paid his respects at Kanda Shrine.{{cite web|title=Visiting Japan In May? Enjoy The Kanda Festival In Tokyo! {{!}} MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|url=http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|website=MOSHI MOSHI NIPPON {{!}} もしもしにっぽん|access-date=3 September 2017|language=ja|date=17 April 2017|archive-date=3 September 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903121213/http://www.moshimoshi-nippon.jp/37668|url-status=live}} Due in part to the proximity of the Kanda Shrine to {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|qid=Q418096|lt=Akihabara|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|qid=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. ==History== Kanda Shrine was first built in the second year of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|qid=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} Era (730 [[:en:AD|AD]]), in the fishing village of Shibasaki, near the modern {{ill|Ōtemachi|en|Ōtemachi|qid=Q1332496|lt=Ōtemachi|1=Ōtemachi}} district. In order to accommodate the expansion of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|qid=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}, the shrine was later moved to the former Kanda ward in 1603, then moved once again to its modern site on a small hill near {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|qid=Q418096|lt=Akihabara|1=Akihabara}} in 1616. The shrine has been rebuilt and restored many times. The current structure was destroyed in the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|1923 Great Kantō earthquake]] and rebuilt in 1934 with concrete, and thus survived the Tokyo {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|qid=Q59886|lt=firebombing|1=Bombing of Tokyo}} of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, unlike many of Japan's historical structures. Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due to its proximity to the {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|qid=Q418096|lt=Akihabara Electric Town|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for the {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|qid=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda Shrine sells {{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|qid=Q1756788|lt=talismans|1=Omamori}} specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp|access-date=2015-12-05|archive-date=2015-12-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151208062432/http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|url-status=live}} ==Architecture== The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|qid=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} wood, and is built with an [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, and {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|qid=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}}. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[:en:Seven Gods of Fortune|Seven Gods of Fortune]] (Japanese, "Shichifukujin"), Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado was a land-owning government official who led a massive insurrection against the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} government and declared himself the "New Emperor" (新皇). He was later elevated to the status of a local kami out of a mixture of fear and reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his defeat in 940 AD, he was {{ill|Decapitation|en|Decapitation|qid=Q204933|lt=decapitated|1=Decapitation}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Hidesato|en|Fujiwara no Hidesato|qid=Q1136623|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}} and his severed head was brought to the Shibasaki ({{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}) area in a wooden bucket (首桶) and buried on a low hill near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance, and fearing his curse, enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as a dangerous anti-government {{ill|Demagogue|en|Demagogue|qid=Q10264925|lt=Demagogue|1=Demagogue}}. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]]. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[:en:Festivals of Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|qid=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every {{ill|Parity (mathematics)|en|Parity (mathematics)|qid=Q230967|lt=odd|1=Parity (mathematics)}} year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the {{ill|Anime|en|Anime|qid=Q1107|lt=Anime|1=Anime}} and multimedia franchise ''{{ill|Love Live! School Idol Project|en|Love Live! School Idol Project|qid=Q3280971|lt=Love Live! School Idol Project|1=Love Live! School Idol Project}}'', the character Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine, while she and the other members of protagonist idol group μ's train by running up and down stairs leading to the shrine. In 2015, the shrine's administrators named Nozomi as an official mascot.{{cite tweet|user=kanda_myoujin|author=神田明神|number=551083827108380673|date=2 January 2015|title=TOKYO MXテレビで、アニメ「ラブライブ！」が、1月4日23時から放送されます。 神田明神公式アイドルの「東條 希」が出演していて、神田明神もアニメ中に出てきます。 僕、ししまいも応援していますよm(_ _)m}} ==images== File:Kanda-Myojin torii.JPG|[[Torii]] File:Daikoku8727.jpg|[[Daikokuten]] File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji File:Kanda Matsuri 2009-1 in Akihabara.jpg|{{ill|Kanda Matsuri|en|Kanda Matsuri|qid=Q218646|lt=Kanda Matsuri|1=Kanda Matsuri}} File:100 views edo 010.jpg|[[Hiroshige]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons Category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] [[en:Kanda Shrine]] [[arz:كاندا ميوجين]] {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin}} [[de:Kanda Myōjin]] [[eu:Kanda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد کاندا]] [[fr:Kanda-myōjin]] [[id:Kuil Kanda]] [[it:Santuario di Kanda]] [[ja:神田明神]] [[pl:Kanda-myōjin]] [[zh:神田明神]] {{wikidata link|Q717682}} [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiyoda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1934]] [[Category:Shinto in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Restoration is being done on Kanda Shrine, and work continues today. Due to its proximity to the {{ill|Akihabara|en|Akihabara|qid=Q418096|lt=Akihabara Electric Town|1=Akihabara}}, the shrine has become a mecca for the {{ill|Technophilia|en|Technophilia|qid=Q1658941|lt=technophiles|1=Technophilia}} who frequent Akihabara. The Kanda Shrine sells {{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|qid=Q1756788|lt=talismans|1=Omamori}} specifically for blessing electronic devices against the types of harm that could come to them.{{cite web|title=Kandamyoujin shrine sells IT talismans|url=http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|website=KandaMyoujin.or.jp|access-date=2015-12-05|archive-date=2015-12-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151208062432/http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/omamori/detail.html?id=7|url-status=live}} ==Architecture== The two-storey main gate, Zuishin-mon (隨神門), marks the entrance to Kanda Shrine. Zuishin-mon was reconstructed in 1995 with {{ill|Cypress|en|Cypress|qid=Q14169641|lt=Cypress|1=Cypress}} wood, and is built with an [[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]] styled roof. The shrine building is constructed in the Shinto style of [[Gongen|Gongen-zukuri]]. It is painted vermilion, and decorated with gold and lacquered interiors. Many sculptures of its enshrined [[kami]] can be found on the building grounds. ==Enshrined Kami== The three major kami enshrined are [[Daikokuten]], {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, and {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|qid=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}}. As Daikokuten and Ebisu both belong to the [[:en:Seven Gods of Fortune|Seven Gods of Fortune]] (Japanese, "Shichifukujin"), Kanda Shrine is a popular place for businessmen and entrepreneurs to pray for wealth and prosperity. Taira no Masakado was a land-owning government official who led a massive insurrection against the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} government and declared himself the "New Emperor" (新皇). He was later elevated to the status of a local kami out of a mixture of fear and reverence. He is an important figure in the shrine's history. After his defeat in 940 AD, he was {{ill|Decapitation|en|Decapitation|qid=Q204933|lt=decapitated|1=Decapitation}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Hidesato|en|Fujiwara no Hidesato|qid=Q1136623|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}} and his severed head was brought to the Shibasaki ({{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}) area in a wooden bucket (首桶) and buried on a low hill near the shrine's location today. Locals who respected his defiance, and fearing his curse, enshrined him in Kanda Shrine, and his spirit is said to watch over the surrounding areas. It was rumored that when his shrine fell into disrepair, Masakado's angry spirit wrought natural disasters and plagues upon the nearby lands. It is also said that Tokugawa Ieyasu felt uncomfortable to have his castle built close to such a powerful spirit, and so decided to move Kanda Shrine to its modern location. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} was faced with public pressure to include Kanda Shrine in the {{nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}}, but hesitated to do so because of the shrine's association with Taira no Masakado, who was seen as a dangerous anti-government {{ill|Demagogue|en|Demagogue|qid=Q10264925|lt=Demagogue|1=Demagogue}}. This was temporarily resolved by removing Taira no Masakado as an enshrined kami. However, Masakado's spirit proved so popular amongst the commoners, that it was symbolically returned to the shrine after the [[:en:Second World War|Second World War]]. ==Festivals== {{main|Kanda Matsuri}} Kanda festival (Kanda Matsuri) is one of the three major [[Shinto]] [[:en:Festivals of Tokyo|festivals]] of Tokyo, started in 1600 by Tokugawa Ieyasu to celebrate his decisive victory at the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|qid=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. At the time, the festival was important enough to be named a state festival, and its highly decorated [[mikoshi]] were paraded down the main streets and into Edo castle, so that even the ''shōgun'' could observe the celebrations. Today, it is held in honor of the enshrined kami, and celebrated around May 15 of every {{ill|Parity (mathematics)|en|Parity (mathematics)|qid=Q230967|lt=odd|1=Parity (mathematics)}} year. Daikoku festival is also held at Kanda Shrine in January. ==Cultural references== In the {{ill|Anime|en|Anime|qid=Q1107|lt=Anime|1=Anime}} and multimedia franchise ''{{ill|Love Live! School Idol Project|en|Love Live! School Idol Project|qid=Q3280971|lt=Love Live! School Idol Project|1=Love Live! School Idol Project}}'', the character Nozomi Tojo serves as a [[miko|shrine maiden]] at the Kandamyoujin shrine, while she and the other members of protagonist idol group μ's train by running up and down stairs leading to the shrine. In 2015, the shrine's administrators named Nozomi as an official mascot.{{cite tweet|user=kanda_myoujin|author=神田明神|number=551083827108380673|date=2 January 2015|title=TOKYO MXテレビで、アニメ「ラブライブ！」が、1月4日23時から放送されます。 神田明神公式アイドルの「東條 希」が出演していて、神田明神もアニメ中に出てきます。 僕、ししまいも応援していますよm(_ _)m}} ==images== File:Kanda-Myojin torii.JPG|[[Torii]] File:Daikoku8727.jpg|[[Daikokuten]] File:Kanda shrine paper lantern.jpg|Paper Lantern File:KandaMyojin8816.jpg|{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} Pavilion File:Kanda shrine.jpg|The [[Honden]] File:KandaMyojinLanterns8758.jpg|Lanterns File:KandaMyojinRooflines8768.jpg|Roof Designs File:KandaMyojinRailOrnaments8756.jpg|Rail Ornamentation File:KandaMyojinZenigataHeiji8801.jpg|Monument to Zenigata Heiji File:Kanda Matsuri 2009-1 in Akihabara.jpg|{{ill|Kanda Matsuri|en|Kanda Matsuri|qid=Q218646|lt=Kanda Matsuri|1=Kanda Matsuri}} File:100 views edo 010.jpg|[[Hiroshige]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * [http://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{Commons Category|Kanda-Myojin|Kanda Shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[image:KandaMyojinGate8837.jpg|thumb|left|270px|Zuishin-mon]] {{Commons category|Kanda-Myojin}} {{wikidata link|Q717682|arz|كاندا ميوجين|co|commons:Category:Kanda-Myojin|de|Kanda Myōjin|en|Kanda Shrine|eu|Kanda santutegia|fa|معبد کاندا|fr|Kanda-myōjin|id|Kuil Kanda|it|Santuario di Kanda|ja|神田明神|pl|Kanda-myōjin|simple|Kanda Myōjin Shrine|zh|神田明神}} [[Category:1934 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiyoda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Fu-sha]] [[Category:Kanda, Tokyo]] [[Category:Rebuilt buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1934]] [[Category:Shinto in Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
